title: Business Etiquette & Formality
content: Lebanese business culture blends Western efficiency with Middle Eastern relationship-building. First impressions matter enormously, and personal rapport must be established before getting down to business.

items.label: Greetings matter
items.value: Always greet everyone individually. A firm handshake, eye contact, and كيفك/كيفِك before business talk. Jumping straight to business is considered rude.

items.label: أستاذ / أستاذة (ustāz / ustāze)
items.value: The default respectful title in professional settings. Used for teachers, bosses, and anyone you want to show respect to.

items.label: Business over coffee
items.value: Meetings almost always start with coffee or tea. Declining is awkward. The real negotiation often happens during this informal phase.

items.label: Dress code
items.value: Lebanese business culture is quite formal — suits and polished appearance are the norm. Lebanon is image-conscious even in casual settings.

items.label: إن شاء الله in business
items.value: When a business partner says إن شاء الله (God willing) about a deadline, it often means 'maybe' or 'don't count on it'. Push gently for concrete dates.

title: Code-Switching: Arabic, French & English
content: Lebanon is trilingual. Most educated Lebanese switch seamlessly between Arabic, French, and English — sometimes within a single sentence. This isn't confusion; it's a cultural superpower and a mark of Lebanese identity.

items.label: Hi, kifak, ça va?
items.value: The famous trilingual greeting — English 'hi', Arabic 'how are you', French 'how's it going' — all in one breath. This is peak Lebanese.

items.label: French-dominant sectors
items.value: Medicine, law, and banking often operate in French. Prescriptions are written in French. Many legal terms are French.

items.label: English-dominant sectors
items.value: Technology, business, and marketing tend toward English. LinkedIn profiles are in English. Tech startups use English internally.

items.label: When to use what
items.value: With family: mostly Arabic. At work: Arabic + English/French. On social media: Arabizi or English. Formal writing: MSA (فصحى).

items.label: Don't overthink it
items.value: As a learner, don't worry about code-switching perfectly. Lebanese people appreciate any effort. Mixing languages is natural and expected.

title: Lebanese Humor & Sarcasm
content: Lebanese humor is sharp, ironic, and often dark. Sarcasm is a national pastime, and the ability to crack jokes — especially during hardship — is deeply admired. Understanding humor is the gateway to true cultural fluency.

items.label: Dark humor as survival
items.value: During crises (war, economic collapse, power cuts), Lebanese people joke about their situation. It's a coping mechanism and a cultural strength.

items.label: والله؟ (wallah?)
items.value: Really? / I swear? — said with different tones: genuine surprise, sarcasm, disbelief, or mild mockery. Tone is everything.

items.label: Exaggeration is normal
items.value: عم بموت من الجوع (ʕam bmūt min ij-jūʕ / I'm dying of hunger) and بدي أنفجر (baddi infijir / I'm going to explode) are everyday expressions, not emergencies.

items.label: Political jokes
items.value: Lebanese joke about politicians constantly. It's a safe social topic that everyone bonds over, regardless of political affiliation.

items.label: Self-deprecation
items.value: Making fun of oneself and one's country is common and appreciated. It shows confidence and cultural awareness.
